mnemonics.title: FURTEMP - Quality Attributes
mnemonics.content: FURTEMP stands for Functionality, Usability, Reliability, Testability, Efficiency, Maintainability, Portability - the 8 quality characteristics in ISO/IEC 25010

mnemonics.title: V-Model Journey
mnemonics.content: Think of V-Model as a valley: start at the top-left with requirements, go down to coding at the bottom, then climb up the right side - each step on the left has a corresponding testing phase on the right

mnemonics.title: QA vs QC - The Chef Analogy
mnemonics.content: QA (Quality Assurance) is like the kitchen manager ensuring proper recipes and procedures are followed - it's process-oriented. QC (Quality Control) is like the food critic tasting the final dish - it's product-oriented

mnemonics.title: Verification vs Validation - When?
mnemonics.content: Verification asks 'Are we building the product right?' (during development). Validation asks 'Are we building the right product?' (at the end)

mnemonics.title: PDCA Cycle - Plan Do Check Act
mnemonics.content: Picture a circle: Plan your process, Do implement it, Check measure results, Act on improvements - then repeat forever

mnemonics.title: Testing Levels - Unit Int Sys UAT
mnemonics.content: U.I.S.U: Unit testing (individual components) → Integration testing (combined modules) → System testing (complete system) → User Acceptance Testing (real-world use)

acronyms.usage: Process-oriented activities to ensure quality during development - focuses on prevention of defects
Product-oriented activities to detect defects - focuses on finding bugs in the final product
Verification = checking against specifications; Validation = checking against user needs
Framework to improve software processes; levels: Initial, Managed, Defined, Quantitatively Managed, Optimizing
Organization-wide approach to continuous quality improvement involving all employees
Iterative quality improvement cycle used in process improvement
Set of activities ensuring software process conforms to established standards
Final testing phase where end-users validate the software meets their needs
Test design technique testing values at boundaries of input domains
Test design technique dividing input data into equivalence classes

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a valley shape: requirements on top-left slope descending to coding at the bottom, then testing phases climbing up the right slope - each left phase has a matching right phase (Unit Test ↔ Coding, Integration Test ↔ System Design, etc.)
Picture a triangle: wide base = many Unit tests (fast, cheap), middle = Integration tests, narrow top = End-to-End tests (few, slow, expensive) - this shows test distribution strategy
Visualize a continuous circle with four stations: Plan (draw a map) → Do (walk the path) → Check (look at your position) → Act (adjust direction) - always moving clockwise for improvement
Think of a state machine: New → Assigned → Open → Fixed → Verified → Closed, with paths back to Open if tests fail
Picture a house: Foundation = Functional Suitability, Pillars = Reliability, Usability, Performance Efficiency, Support pillars = Maintainability, Portability, with roof = Security

analogies.analogy: Like a construction supervisor ensuring workers follow proper building codes and procedures before any structural work begins - preventing problems rather than fixing them later
Like a building inspector visiting the finished site to check if everything meets code - examining the final product for defects
Like testing individual bricks for strength before building a wall - checking each component in isolation
Like checking if the electrical wiring connects properly to the circuit breaker and switches - testing how components work together
Like a customer testing a new phone by using apps and features without knowing internal workings - testing based on requirements only
Like a mechanic diagnosing a car engine problem by examining internal components and wiring - testing with full knowledge of code structure
Like after fixing a leaky pipe, turning on all faucets to ensure no new leaks appeared elsewhere - retesting unchanged features
Like a recipe: specifies exact ingredients (inputs), cooking steps (procedures), and expected taste (expected output) for testing software
